reasoning:
The Chicago Fed's National Financial Conditions Risk Subindex (NFCIRISK) has shown a strong, reliable downward trend over the past 6-12 months, indicating progressively looser financial risk conditions. As of late October 2025, the index sits at -0.576, essentially flattening over the preceding two weeks (Oct 17 to Oct 24). 

For the very short-term horizon (Nov 2, roughly 1 week ahead), the momentum still favors slightly looser or unchanged conditions, as systemic shifts take time to materialize; however, the recent flattening lifts the probability of a minor technical bounce. Thus, my estimate is somewhat below 50% (0.35). 

As the forecast horizon extends to ~1 month (Nov 25) and ~3 months (Jan 24), the uncertainty increases substantially. The current reading of -0.576 is historically low (loose), making mean reversion upward to tighter conditions progressively more likely over time. Any unforeseen volatility shocks—such as changing global trade dynamics or macroeconomic data—could easily cause an uptick in risk. Thus, the probabilities increase over time, settling near a coin toss (0.48) for the longest horizon.
